name: Baalbek Trilithon
description: The Baalbek Trilithon refers to three colossal stone blocks forming part of the Temple of Jupiter in Baalbek, Lebanon. Each block weighs approximately 800 tons. Proponents of alternative theories suggest the Trilithon's immense size and precision placement are evidence of advanced, possibly pre-historic or extraterrestrial, technology beyond the capabilities of the Romans or previous civilizations. They often cite the lack of definitive explanations for how the stones were quarried, transported, and lifted into position using only Roman-era technology. Some theories propose the involvement of giants, lost civilizations like Atlantis, or even extraterrestrial beings. Mainstream archaeology attributes the Trilithon to Roman engineering prowess, albeit acknowledging the significant logistical challenges. They propose that the stones were quarried nearby and moved using ramps, levers, and a large workforce. While the exact methods remain debated, researchers point to Roman construction techniques and the availability of resources as plausible explanations, dismissing claims of advanced technology or supernatural intervention as lacking credible evidence. The unfinished state of other large stones in the Baalbek quarry provides further insight into the process, suggesting the project was abandoned before completion, potentially due to engineering difficulties or political upheaval.
subtitle: Colossal Stones of Ancient Baalbek

claims.claim: The Trilithon's weight and precision exceed Roman capabilities.
claims.evidence: Lack of detailed historical records explaining the specific methods used; sheer scale of the stones.
claims.criticism: Roman engineering was highly advanced; evidence of ramps and levers; large workforce available; unfinished stones in the quarry provide clues.

claims.claim: Advanced pre-historic or extraterrestrial technology was used in its construction.
claims.evidence: Lack of conventional explanations; perceived impossibility of moving such massive stones with known Roman technology.
claims.criticism: No credible evidence of such technology exists; Roman engineering, while challenging, is demonstrably capable of large-scale construction.

claims.claim: The Trilithon is a remnant of a lost, more advanced civilization.
claims.evidence: The Trilithon's superior construction compared to other Roman structures at Baalbek; its enigmatic purpose.
claims.criticism: The Trilithon is part of a larger Roman temple complex; the entire complex was built over time with varying degrees of skill and purpose.

keyFigures.name: Erich von Däniken
keyFigures.role: Popularized the idea of extraterrestrial involvement in ancient monuments.

keyFigures.name: Jean-Pierre Adam
keyFigures.role: Archaeologist who studied Roman construction techniques and their application to Baalbek.

timeline.event: Construction of the Temple of Jupiter, including the Trilithon, begins.
Alternative theories about the Trilithon's origins gain popularity.

sources.author: David Macaulay
sources.work: City: A Story of Roman Planning and Construction
sources.title: David Macaulay
